chapters.title: The dark secrets of radiant success
chapters.text:
The fear of death is a universal human experience, as no one truly knows what will happen, and this reality can unsettle even the bravest souls. Just as civilizations, organizations, and companies eventually meet their end, humanity has long sought ways to leave an imperishable legacy throughout history. That is what the developers of Mercedes achieved in 1886 and Coco Chanel in 1910. They didn't know that their products would become synonymous with luxury and wealth a century later, but they had a vision and worked tirelessly to make it influential.

One of the most important steps you can take in building a visionary company is not an action, but a shift in perspective. ~ Jim Collins, Jerry I. Porras

No matter how great a boss you are, a company's history depends on the contributions of many individuals. That's why focusing on the future, not just the immediate gains, and applying the clock-building approach is a far wiser choice. To better grasp this concept, imagine someone skilled at accurately telling time using the sun while camping. It's an impressive skill, but it could be even more valuable if they built a device to continue that function even after their departure.

People are mortal, but ideas are eternal.

For example, the Founding Fathers of the American Constitution didn’t merely react to their current circumstances but built a system to raise a new generation of bright leaders. Visionary companies operate similarly, surrounded by fame and potentially harmful myths that can impede those beginning this journey. Let's destroy them:

• The myth of the big idea: Only a few visionary companies began with a big idea; many started with failures.
• The myth of the charismatic leader: Don't feel like a superstar? It doesn't matter; many CEOs of top companies, such as Masaru Ibuka from Sony, were shy and reserved.
• The myth of profit maximization: Money is a company's lifeblood, but ideas and values must remain its backbone for longevity since a good reputation is priceless.

With these myths dispelled, you can now see the future you desire more clearly. So don't wait for the right moment to realize your dreams; create them.

chapters.title: How to become immortal
chapters.text:

chapters.title: Not “or” but “and”: why you should think bigger
chapters.text:
The human brain often falls into the trap of “or.” For instance, young entrepreneurs might feel compelled to choose between growth or stability, conservatism or courage, or low price or high quality. Take a deep breath and clear your mind: you don't have to do this. Instead of being ruled by “or,” implement “and” as visionary companies do.

The practice of “and instead of or” helps you avoid boxing yourself in and allows you to see the bigger picture. For example, your primary goal might be a long-term investment, but short-term performance can still affect it. Your company can be conservative and adapt to changes quickly. This way of thinking raises the bar of expectations and creates new ideas to implement the business's core ideology.

Train your mind to believe in high achievements to overcome the most significant difficulties.

The core ideology embodies the values and ideals important to the company. They are similar to the foundation of a house that remains unchanged no matter how often you repaint the walls or renovate the roof. The principles of implementing the ideology may change, and the company may rebrand, but the heart of the company stays the same.

For example, one of the largest grocery corporations in the United States, Walmart, believes in exceeding customer expectations. Having greeters at the store entrances was just one way to demonstrate this, so when they abandoned it, their decision didn't make them any less popular, but instead showed flexibility and a desire to improve.

Visionary companies use various methods to stay true to their core and drive progress. Here are some of them:

• Big Hairy Audacious Goals (BHAGs): Setting big, challenging goals to drive progress
• Cult-like cultures: Fostering a shared experience of ideology that employees embrace
• Experimentation: Trying different approaches and holding onto what works
• Home-grown management: Promoting those aligned with the company's core ideology
• Good enough is never enough: Continuously striving for improvement, no matter how successful you become

Try using these tips gradually, and you'll begin to dream even more — and, most importantly, make even your wildest dreams come true.

chapters.title: Shoot for the moon to achieve the stars
chapters.text:
There's a big difference between having a vision and implementing it. Even when you have clearly defined your goal, there can be various obstacles on the way, which are difficult for any company to overcome. However, if there is enthusiasm and good relations within the team, a daunting task won't be seen as a test but as a boost for growth. The Boeing Company faced such a situation in 1952.

The company's engineers proposed building a large commercial airplane, an ambitious project considering Boeing's cargo products had no market presence at the time. Moreover, airlines expressed little interest, and Boeing needed more funds to create a prototype. However, the team was so passionate about the idea that they persisted, working tirelessly to create the legendary 707. This model was a real breakthrough in commercial aviation, making Boeing an undisputed market leader for a long time.

Your ideas will gain more value when you show them to the world.

Let's think about your Big Hairy Audacious Goals (BHAGs). To develop the company, it should meet several crucial factors:

• It must be clear and understandable for each team member.
• The goal should be interesting enough to inspire passion in people's hearts.
• Once achieved, there should be other goals to prevent stagnation.
• It must be aligned with the company's core values, ensuring you can find the right people among your employees to implement it, even in your absence.

Compatibility with employees' values is crucial because it also impacts relationships within the company. For example, Sony values promoting Japanese culture and innovation. Employees who shared this desire improved Japan's image worldwide and developed the first pocket transistor radio. At the same time, those with differing priorities would have struggled in Sony's intense corporate culture, so the company was selective in hiring, eliminating many highly qualified personnel.

A team united by a shared vision can move mountains and will likely have positive working relationships. Look for those who share your goals because they will be motivated to perform as efficiently as possible.

Did you know? In 1990, Sony released 500 new products, meaning the company invented nearly two new products daily.

chapters.title: The solution for your evolution
chapters.text:
Most visionary companies do not stick to a rigid, step-by-step plan to achieve their goals yet still outperform their competitors. How? The secret formula for their success lies in their flexibility. Visionary companies have a broad strategy but like to experiment, make mistakes, and take advantage of opportunities to determine what will work. As a result, they achieve groundbreaking innovations, as shown by Johnson & Johnson (J&J).

J&J began as a manufacturer of antiseptics and medical bandages until Fred Kilmer, the director of research, suggested including a small can of talcum powder in every package of products. This powder helped reduce irritation caused by wearing medical bandages. To the company's surprise, they received hundreds of grateful responses from patients and dozens of orders for the talc. J&J responded by creating a line of products for sensitive skin, especially for children, and the result was remarkable. Their powder became globally famous, accounting for 44% of the company's profits and setting the stage for its development decades later.

Your failures are part of your evolutionary process, so don't be shy about them.

You can describe this kind of progress as evolutionary, as companies transform due to external factors, much like species in Darwin's theory of evolution. Evolutionary progress is a healthy and logical process that separates those with a vision from those without. It differs from the BHAGs approach because the company doesn't take giant leaps toward the planned goal but responds to challenges in small steps.

Sticking to the core during your evolution, not the products, is essential for survival. You can see it clearly in the race between Motorola and Zenith, two companies that produced radio and television equipment. Motorola was constantly expanding its horizons, entering the new era of digital technology with a bang. In contrast, Zenith continued to do what it was already good at and went bankrupt during the transition to the 21st century.

Your core also determines how people will remember you. Walt Disney had no prior experience building amusement parks, but he wanted to showcase his creations to the world in a new form. His cherished Disney World has captured the hearts of countless children and adults alike. So, don't hesitate to celebrate what you love.

chapters.title: Tips to find the top talent without going crazy
chapters.text:
Supertasks require a team of superheroes, so every company needs to find top professionals to lead its projects. This task is challenging because the ideal candidate must have good skills, work well with the team, and fit the corporate culture. Checking each point can become quite an undertaking, and the result will only be apparent after a project or two, which is risky. That is why visionary companies often grow their talent like gardeners raise plants.

As a mentor, you develop both yourself and others.

Based on Jim Collins and Jerry I. Porras' research, about 72% of visionary companies gave top positions to specialists within the company. This means that even when hiring fresh blood, they planted the seeds for developing young talent who would boldly be entrusted with the future. This is important because only continuity of sound leadership can guarantee success, and the only way to create such a system is by constantly improving employees' skills.

You can build a visionary company. ~ Jim Collins, Jerry I. Porras

Every minute invested in training will pay off with big profits later; visionary companies understand this. Motorola, for example, targets 40 hours of training per week for every worker annually and requires that each division spends 1.5% of its payroll on training. Instead of waiting for the world to inevitably change, they aggressively invest in development, try new management methods, and upgrade their employees' skills to stay ahead. Running a business is like running on a treadmill: if you stand still, you will fall behind.

What happens if a company doesn't allow talent to grow? At best, employees will leave and join competitors; at worst, this approach can lead to the company’s downfall. Colgate, once the leader of the toothpaste market, lost over half its size and three-quarters of its profits in the 1940s. The reason for this was poor leadership choices. The company's star executives had aged, and other family members lacked the skills to be effective managers but refused to step down. Ultimately, Colgate lost the race to P&G, which still owns many well-known brands, such as Tide laundry detergent.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Vision separates market leaders from those who will be left behind. It helps a company achieve short-term success and become nearly immortal by raising the next generations of loyal managers. Visionary companies raise these talents by cherishing their core values. They set big goals that stimulate progress and evolve in response to market challenges. If you want to create a visionary company, take the first step today.

Try this

• Make a list of your values: A leader can only build a company around a core that is personally important to them.
• Dream up a big goal: Even if it seems daunting, a bold vision will encourage you to grow and take steps to achieve it.
• Find interesting courses: A leader should always be one step ahead of even the most talented employees. The better your team is, the more personal development you need to do.

Read it till the end?
